data.title: Nomura Research Institute FY Profit Declines
data.description:
BRUSSELS/FRANKFURT/PARIS (dpa-AFX) - Nomura Research Institute (NR7.F, 4307.T) reported fiscal year profit attributable to owners of parent of 15.3 billion yen compared to 93.8 billion yen, prior year. Profit per share was 26.62 yen compared to 163.56 yen. For the fiscal year ended March 31, 2026, revenue was 814.71 billion yen, up 6.5% from last year.

For the fiscal year ending March 31, 2027, the company expects: revenue of 850.0 billion yen; profit attributable to owners of parent of 119.0 billion yen; and basic earnings per share of 207.17 yen.

Shares of Nomura Research Institute are trading at 5,092 yen, up 0.33%

data.title: Porsche to Sell Its Stakes in Bugatti Joint Venture and Rimac — Update
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping

Porsche agreed to sell its stakes in hypercar joint venture Bugatti Rimac and electric-vehicle maker Rimac Group, moving to focus on its core business as it continues to work through a turnaround program.

Porsche and Rimac established the Bugatti Rimac joint venture in 2021 to house the Bugatti brand, with Porsche holding a 45% share and Rimac owning the remaining 55%.

Porsche also holds a 20.6% stake in Rimac.

The deal announced Friday will see Porsche sell both of those stakes to a consortium led by U.S.-based investment firm HOF Capital, and which includes Abu Dhabi-based BlueFive Capital as its largest investor as well as institutional investors across the U.S. and European Union.

Financial terms weren't disclosed.

"With the sale of our stake, we demonstrate that we will focus Porsche on the core business," Michael Leiters, chief executive of the German luxury sports-car maker, said.

Former McLaren boss Leiters took over at Porsche at the start of this year after a series of guidance downgrades last year as the automaker grappled with a slow uptake of electric vehicles, weakness in China, and U.S. tariffs.

He has begun laying out plans to turn Porsche's fortunes around through streamlining the management structure, cutting hierarchies, and reducing bureaucracy to create a leaner and faster organization and boost the desirability of its products.

The company is investing in new gas-powered and hybrid models after deciding to delay the rollout of new all-electric cars--part of a product-realignment plan that saw the company book around 2.4 billion euros ($2.8 billion) in one-off costs last year.

Friday's deal is expected to complete by year-end, with Rimac taking control of Bugatti Rimac and creating a strategic partnership with HOF Capital and BlueFive Capital to support the company's next phase of growth.

HOF Capital will become Rimac's largest shareholder alongside Mate Rimac, founder of Rimac and chief executive of Bugatti Rimac.

Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com

data.title: Infrastructure Investments in Southeast Asia Expected to Keep Growing — Market Talk
data.description:
0919 GMT - Infrastructure investments in Asia is expected to keep growing in the next decade, Bain & Company says in its latest report. Many opportunities are linked to data centers, where the number of deals in the region rose over 12 times from 2018 to 2025, it says. Malaysia, Thailand and Indonesia are forecast to drive most of the growth within Southeast Asia. "Even if you put aside AI, Southeast Asia as a region is still well below its full potential when it comes to cloud penetration," said Usman Akhtar, senior partner of Bain & Co., in a briefing. The primary demand driver for data center capacity has been hyperscalers who take up long-term contracts, he said. "We see this continued push by the hyperscalers to... accelerate their AI capex buildouts, and it's a global thing," he says. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0919 GMT - Uncertainty around Japan's inflation outlook backs the case for the Bank of Japan to stay in wait-and-see mode next week, HSBC economists say. While March headline CPI remained below target as subsidies restrained fuel and utility costs, the more important signal was in "core-core inflation," which excludes fresh food and energy. That measure remained sticky, in line with price hikes by food and beverage manufacturers due to higher input costs. If the Middle East conflict persists, higher energy and fertilizer prices--plus freight and insurance charges and a stronger dollar--could swell Japan's food costs. That's souring household sentiment, and raising inflation expectations. HSBC's base case remains for one 25bp hike in July to 1.00%, with the odds of an earlier move rising if the conflict persists beyond mid-May. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0854 GMT - U.K. retail sales data for March was stronger than expected, lowering the probability of the Bank of England cutting interest rates in the coming months, eToro's Lale Akoner says in a note. Monthly retail sales rose 0.7% in March, above the 0.1% consensus forecast by economists in a WSJ poll. "Overall, it seems that consumption is holding up for now, but quality of demand is weak and increasingly tied to higher essential spending," Akoner says. Investors fully price in the possibility of two quarter-point interest-rate rises by the BOE in 2026, LSEG data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)"U.S. Treasurys sold off a touch as oil drifted higher despite the ongoing cease-fire," Barclays's Anshul Pradhan and Demi Hu say in a note. "U.S. Treasury Yields Edge Up as Oil Price Remain High — Market Talk," at 0529 GMT, misspelled Pradhan's name.

0851 GMT - The Swiss franc could weaken further if the Swiss National Bank lags other central banks in raising interest rates in response to higher energy prices pushing up inflation, ING's Chris Turner says in a note. Switzerland is less exposed to the oil price shock stemming from the Iran war as hydro, nuclear, and solar make up a large part of its electricity production. That means the SNB will be one of the last central banks to increase rates, he says. Higher oil prices will see European Central Bank rates repriced higher than SNB rates, potentially lifting the euro against the franc, he says. The euro rises 0.1% to 0.9195 francs and ING expects it to reach 0.9250-0.9260. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0848 GMT - As risks intendify, the Indonesian rupiah could weaken to 17,650 against the dollar by end-2026 from a previous estimate of 17,400, RHB strategists Muhamad Farid Anas Bin Johari and Wong Xian Yong say in a note. Persistent capital outflows, rising fiscal pressures from higher energy subsidies and elevated global risk aversion are weighing on sentiment, they say. Foreign investors have continued selling Indonesian equities and bonds, while government bond yields have risen on higher risk premiums. Concerns over Indonesia's investability, including MSCI's extended review of market reforms, are also dampening inflows, they add. Ongoing geopolitical tensions and high oil prices may further strain fiscal space, limiting policy flexibility and reinforcing downside pressure on the rupiah, RHB adds. The dollar falls 0.2% to 17257.50 rupiah. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)The dollar rises after President Trump announced a three-week extension to the cease-fire between Israel and Lebanon but said he was in no rush to secure a long-term peace deal with Iran. "Dollar Rises as Uncertainty Over Iran War Persists — Market Talk," at 0642 GMT, misstated that Trump announced an extension to the cease-fire with Iran. The error also appeared in "Sterling Shows Little Reaction to U.K. Retail Sales Data; Focus Stays on Iran War — Market Talk," at 0649 GMT, and "Bitcoin Falls as Iran War Uncertainty Keeps Investors Cautious — Market Talk," at 0656 GMT.

0842 GMT - German 10-year Bund yields edge higher and the euro is little changed after Germany's Ifo data. The business climate index was worse than expected while expectations weakened in April. The Ifo sentiment index was 84.4 in April, below analysts' forecasts of 85.5 in the Wall Street Journal's poll and also below March's 86.3. The 10-year Bund yield trades at 3.037%, up 3 basis points after the data, having traded at 3.032% beforehand, according to Tradeweb. The euro remains steady against the dollar at $1.1688, little changed after the data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0807 GMT - U.K. retail sales data for March were stronger than expected, indicating that consumers have coped well with the initial shock of the Middle East conflict, Pantheon Macroeconomics' Elliott Jordan-Doak says in a note. Monthly retail sales came in at 0.7% in March, above the 0.1% consensus forecast by economists in a WSJ poll. The data bodes well for activity over the rest of the year, Jordan-Doak says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0805 GMT - Singapore's industrial production likely posted a volatile rebound in March, after marking a contraction in February, DBS's economics team says in a report. "The decline in February was due to temporary shutdowns during the Lunar New Year festive period that largely normalized in March," they say. The expansion in industrial production continues to be driven by robust momentum in electronics production amid tailwinds from global artificial-intelligence demand. DBS expects Singapore to post a 10.0% on-year growth for March industrial production, reversing February's 0.1% contraction.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0758 GMT - The 0.7% rise in U.K. retail volumes in March was driven by a 6.1% on-month rise in fuel sales, a clear sign that consumers were bringing forward fuel purchases ahead of price increases, RSM U.K. economist Thomas Pugh says. Excluding fuel, retail sales increased by only 0.2%. The slump in consumer confidence in April suggests that the Iran war will have a bigger effect on this month's sales, Pugh says in a note. Still, the high household saving rate means consumers can cushion the blow to their lifestyles, indicating the full impact on income is unlikely to flow through into spending, he says. But as the crisis drags on, it sets a much tougher outlook for retailers than before the war, Pugh says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0752 GMT - The euro won't necessarily rise if markets increase bets for the European Central Bank to raise interest rates in June, ING's Chris Turner says in a note. "The ECB needs to get ahead of inflation expectations to help the euro, not merely match them." Two-year real interest-rate differentials adjusted for inflation aren't particularly supportive for the euro versus the dollar rate right now, he says. The market sees a 76% chance of a rate rise in June and is fully pricing in two rises by year-end, LSEG data show. This is in response to higher energy prices due to the Iran war. The euro trades steady at $1.1680. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Inflation Uncertainty Backs Case for BOJ to Stay on Hold — Market Talk
data.description: Uncertainty around Japan's inflation outlook backs the case for the Bank of Japan to stay in wait-and-see mode next week, HSBC economists say. While March headline CPI remained below target as subsidies restrained fuel and utility costs, the more important signal was in "core-core inflation," which excludes fresh food and energy. That measure remained sticky, in line with price hikes by food and beverage manufacturers due to higher input costs. If the Middle East conflict persists, higher energy and fertilizer prices--plus freight and insurance charges and a stronger dollar--could swell Japan's food costs. data.title: Europe's economic momentum is fading fast
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 down 0.9%, set for weekly fall
  • Middle East energy supply remains severely disrupted
  • Impact starting to show in the data
  • SAP rises on profit beat, share buyback
  • Nasdaq futures rise after Intel results

EUROPE'S ECONOMIC MOMENTUM IS FADING FAST

Economic momentum in the euro zone is fading fast. Business activity in the bloc suffered a surprise contraction in April, with the war in the Middle East sapping demand.

And there have been other indicators too. Germany, the euro zone's largest economy, has halved its 2026 GDP growth forecast to 0.5% from 1%, while ECB President Lagarde warned that the growth and inflation outlook is at risk.

Citi's euro zone economic surprise metric, which measures how economic data in the prior three months differs from consensus forecasts, is firmly in negative territory, in contrast to other major economies such as the U.S., China and the UK.

It suggests economists have been too optimistic about the euro zone's economy.

Unsurprisingly, it's the constrained flow of ships, and therefore oil & gas, through the Strait of Hormuz that is causing the economy to struggle.

"Every additional day of disruption shifts the balance of risk towards higher-for-longer energy prices and, eventually, demand destruction," write Barclays equity strategists in a note.

The bigger cost of the war for the euro zone pushed the investment bank to turn underweight Europe versus the U.S. earlier this month, and reiterate their caution on the consumer.

Yet, they don't believe it's all bad.

"We see the war reinforcing the need to accelerate investments in strategic autonomy, which we think remains a compelling medium term investment theme."

data.title: Chinese Equities Likely to See Further Upside Toward Year-End — Market Talk
data.description: Chinese equities have a 5%-10% upside toward the year-end, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a research note. China's e-commere competition is likely to ease following regulatory tightening, and with strengthened positioning in global supply chains--particularly in upstream manufacturing and hard-tech--expected to provide durable support. MS reiterates its year-end target for the Hang Seng Index at 27500. "We expect the path to remain choppy in the near term with volatility persisting through the May to July period," noting uncertainties around the U.S.-China summit, the 1Q earnings season, IPO shares unlocking and the ongoing Middle East situation. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: How SaaSpocalypse fears actually bolstered SAP profits by over $150 million
data.description:
By Steve Goldstein

SAP CEO Christian Klein presided over a quarter in which cloud revenue and backlog both grew rapidly.

For one software giant, the fear that artificial intelligence will render their products obsolete has a silver lining.

SAP's chief financial officer, Dominik Asam, pointed out that the German software giant's steep share-price decline in the first quarter saved it some EUR135 million ($158 million) in compensation.

"The SaaSpocalypse debate and the related 28% decline in our share price during the first quarter alone left its traces in that position," said Asam in a Thursday night call with analysts, according to a FactSet transcript.

"While we hedge the lion's share of our cash-settled grants, the sheer magnitude of the move in the unhedged portion, in combination with related social charges that are not hedged, provided this, I have to admit, unintended relief, adding to continued strong general cost discipline."

SaaSpocalypse is the portmanteau combining the acronym for software-as-a-service, SaaS, with apocalypse. The iShares Expanded Tech-Software ETF IGV, at its lows, has fallen as much as 29% this year on those fears.

SAP stock (SAP) (XE:SAP) rallied 6% in Frankfurt trade as the competitor to Oracle reported 27% growth in cloud revenue and 25% growth in its cloud backlog, both in constant currencies, in the first quarter. The stock however is still down 29% this year.

"This is clearly encouraging and pointing to sustained healthy demand momentum for SAP's solutions," said Deutsche Bank analysts of the first-quarter numbers.

SAP says that AI is going to help its business, pointing out examples such as Daimler Truck using an SAP AI product to improve the win rate with customers and the Queensland, Australian government using it to predict road surface issues across 33,000 kilometers. But CEO Christian Klein did acknowledge that some of the complex AI tasks are only 85% to 90% accurate.

"Is this enough when you are touching the payroll, the finance, the financial close, the supply chains of a customer? No, it's not enough. It's not that the customers don't see the value. They see the value. But we have to go the last mile," he said.

-Steve Goldstein

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Stock Futures Mixed, Oil Gains — WSJ
data.description:
By Caitlin McCabe

Intel is helping brighten a market clouded by war anxiety.

Shares of the chip maker are up more than 20% premarket after the company reported higher sales and raised its guidance amid a resurgence in demand for its CPUs. That's giving Nasdaq-100 futures a boost.

Otherwise, concern over stalled Middle East peace talks continues to weigh on markets. Contracts tied to the Dow industrials are edging down. Oil prices are rising for a fifth straight day, with front-month Brent crude futures trading above $106 a barrel.

Traders entered this week hopeful that Washington and Tehran would convene again for talks in Islamabad. But no such talks have materialized and the Strait of Hormuz still remains effectively shut. In a sign of the deepening standoff, a third U.S. aircraft carrier arrived in waters of the Middle East, giving Trump additional warships to enforce his blockade of Iranian ports and more firepower should he decide to resume strikes on Iran.

The lack of progress has weighed on stocks this week, leaving all three major U.S. indexes in the red through Thursday's close. Brent crude has climbed roughly 12%, putting it on pace for its biggest weekly gain since early March, shortly after the war began.

Procter & Gamble will report results before the opening bell today. A final reading on consumer sentiment is due at 10 a.m. ET.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: China first-quarter fiscal spending quickens as Beijing seeks to spur growth
data.description:
China’s fiscal spending rose 2.6% in the first quarter from a year earlier, accelerating from a 1% increase in 2025, the finance ministry said on Friday, as Beijing steps up support for economic growth amid rising global risks caused by the Middle East conflict.

Fiscal expenditure in the January–March period totalled 7.47 trillion yuan ($1.09 trillion), while fiscal revenue grew 2.4% from a year earlier to 6.16 trillion yuan, the ministry said.

First‑quarter fiscal spending accounted for 24.9% of annual budgeted expenditure - the highest in recent years - a finance ministry official told a media briefing, as authorities pledged to boost government outlays to help meet this year’s economic growth target.

At an agenda-setting meeting last month, Chinese policymakers vowed to continue a "more proactive" fiscal policy for 2026, promising record public spending, government bond issuance and transfers to local government.

Government land sales revenue fell 24.4% over the first three months from a year earlier, the ministry also said.

Revenue from land sales by local governments was down 25.2% on the year over the first two months of 2026, following a 14.7% contraction in 2025.

Local governments have previously relied on the sales of land-use rights to property developers as a major source of revenue. A prolonged property market downturn that started in mid-2021 has continued to strain local government finances.

($1 = 6.8377 Chinese yuan renminbi)

data.title: Southeast Asia's Private-Equity Market Recovery Faces Exit Constraints — Market Talk
data.description: Southeast Asia's private-equity market is stabilizing, but the recovery is narrow and affected by exit constraints, Bain & Co. says. Exit value declined 32% in 2025 in the region compared with a year earlier, but IPO activity showed early signs of recovery. The exit environment became more challenging due to macroeconomic softness and IPO market unpredictability, it says. While there's plenty of capital that still needs to be deployed, currently there's a disconnect between buyers, who are seeing the economic uncertainties as an opportunity to talk down valuations, and sellers, who are arguing that the backdrop is temporary, says Suvir Varma, an advisory partner at Bain & Co. in a media briefing. "It's just a matter of, given all these exogenous factors around us, can buyers and sellers meet on price?" he says. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

data.title: Private-Credit Withdrawals Aren't Just About Fear — WSJ
data.description:
By Jack Pitcher

Some private-credit investors are shifting cash from one kind of debt fund to another, capitalizing on the differences in how they are valued. All of these funds hold private loans, but some are trading at a discount to others. Wall Street has a name for trades of this nature: arbitrage.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: SAP Shares Climb on Cloud Business Resilience
data.description:
By Mauro Orru

SAP shares jumped after the German business-software group reported a strong first quarter that showed the resilience of its cloud business, reassuring investors it can weather potential disruption from artificial intelligence and geopolitical tensions that could affect client spending.

The company behind the Concur travel and expense management platform said its current cloud backlog--a closely watched measure of sales that SAP expects over the coming year based on existing contracts--grew 25% at constant currencies, above expectations of around 24%.

SAP shares in Frankfurt gained more than 7% on Friday. However, the stock is down nearly 30% since the year began. Software stocks have taken a beating in recent months after AI advancements raised fears that the technology could replace services for which companies like SAP charge clients. Investors were also concerned that the conflict in the Middle East could prompt clients to review their spending plans and delay software deals.

However, strong current cloud backlog growth is an encouraging sign that clearly shows demand for SAP's products--including its AI portfolio--remains healthy, Deutsche Bank analysts wrote in a note to clients.

Reporting on a non-IFRS basis, SAP said total revenue increased 12% at constant currencies to nearly 9.56 billion euros ($11.17 billion) from a year earlier. Sales from SAP's core cloud business grew 27% to 5.96 billion euros. Analysts had forecast total revenue of 9.53 billion euros and cloud revenue of 5.87 billion euros, according to a non-IFRS consensus provided by the company.

SAP said its cloud business fared extremely well across Brazil, France, Germany, India, South Korea, Switzerland and the U.K., though performance was also strong in the U.S. Chief Executive Christian Klein said SAP was growing faster than the market and that business AI momentum remained strong as enterprises seek to harness the technology to improve their operations.

"The company emphasised that the growing integration of AI across products will drive a gradual increase in consumption-based cloud revenue over time but that the shift is expected to play out over several years as AI adoption expands across the customer base and should be evolutionary rather than disruptive," Barclays analysts wrote in a research note.

SAP's net profit climbed to 2 billion euros from 1.68 billion euros. Operating profit--a closely watched metric for software companies--rose to 2.87 billion euros from nearly 2.46 billion euros, generating an operating margin of 30%. Analysts had forecast an operating profit of 2.71 billion euros and an operating margin of 28.5%, according to the non-IFRS consensus.

SAP, like other European software companies, presents its figures as two sets of numbers. One set is based on the International Financial Reporting Standards--an international accounting method that seeks to provide a global reporting standard--though analysts and investors tend to follow SAP's non-IFRS numbers, which exclude restructuring expenses and acquisition-related charges.

For the year, SAP continues to expect non-IFRS operating profit between 11.9 billion and 12.3 billion euros, cloud revenue of 25.8 billion to 26.2 billion euros and free cash flow of roughly 10 billion euros.

Write to Mauro Orru at mauro.orru@wsj.com

data.title: China Adds Seven European Companies to Export Control List
data.description:
By Tracy Qu

China's commerce ministry added seven European entities to an export-control list, citing national security reasons.

The move bars Chinese exporters from shipping dual-use items that could have both civilian and military purposes to the named firms.

The ministry announced the move on Friday, saying it takes effect immediately.

Among the companies named in the statement were Belgian weapons manufacturers FN Herstal and FN Browning, German defense company Hensoldt, and Czech company Omnipol, which specializes in high-tech defence and security technologies.

Exporters will need to seek Chinese authorities' approval if they still want to sell to companies on the export-control list, the statement said.

Write to Tracy Qu at tracy.qu@wsj.com

data.title: Key facts: SoftBank Seeks ~$10B OpenAI-Backed Loan; DigitalBridge Buyout OK
data.description:
• SoftBank Group (9984) seeks a ~$10B two-year margin loan backed by its OpenAI stake, following a ~$40B bridge loan in March; sold Nvidia and T‑Mobile shares to raise AI funding.1
  • SoftBank Group (9984) won shareholder approval for its $16.00-per-share all-cash buyout of DigitalBridge; ~69% of shares voted and ~96% of votes were in favor on April 23, 2026.2
